items.q: Does the doctrine of the Trinity mean Christians believe in three gods?
items.correct: No — the Trinity teaches one God in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit), distinct in person but united in one being; Christianity remains monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three separate gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to the three wise men at Jesus' birth.
items.why: Students hear 'three persons' and conclude polytheism; the orthodox teaching insists the three persons share one divine essence.

items.q: What is the difference between omnipotent and omniscient?
items.correct: Omnipotent means all-powerful; omniscient means all-knowing. They are distinct attributes of God, often listed together with omnibenevolence (all-loving).
items.misconception: Omnipotent means God knows everything that will happen.
items.random_wrong: Omnipotent means God exists only in heaven.
items.why: The similar Latin prefixes lead students to swap the meanings; exams frequently test these attribute definitions precisely.

items.q: Can God be both transcendent and immanent?
items.correct: Yes — most theists hold both: God is beyond the universe (transcendent) yet also present and active within it (immanent), for example through answered prayer.
items.misconception: If God is transcendent and outside the universe, God cannot also act within the world.
items.random_wrong: Transcendent and immanent are two different gods in Christianity.
items.why: Students treat the two attributes as contradictory opposites that force a choice, but mainstream theology holds them together as complementary.

items.q: What does it mean to describe God as omnipotent?
items.why: Correct answer: All-powerful. “All-knowing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does 'transcendent' mean when applied to God?
items.correct: Existing beyond space, time and the physical universe
items.misconception: Limited by the laws of nature
items.why: It's Existing beyond space, time and the physical universe. “Limited by the laws of nature” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which term describes belief in one God?
items.why: Correct answer: Monotheism. “Polytheism” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is an example of special revelation?
items.correct: God speaking through scripture or a prophet
items.misconception: Inferring a creator from the beauty of nature
items.random_wrong: Reasoning that the universe had a cause
items.why: Correct answer: God speaking through scripture or a prophet. “Inferring a creator from the beauty of nature” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many persons are there in the Christian Trinity?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 persons. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
